title: Repository Repair, Release, and Collaboration Capstone · Guided Repository Studio
summary: Recover and ship a repository containing mixed work, a conflict, a mistaken commit, a remote update, and a release deadline. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of a complete Git foundations evidence portfolio while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Capture repository boundaries, config origins, current operation, worktree/index state, graph, remotes, and recovery refs before repair.
Order preservation, reproduction, local repair, tests, history cleanup, and publication from least to most irreversible.
Coordinate rewritten history, conflicts, ownership, release changes, and recovery instructions with explicit affected refs and people.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-basics cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Commit to the mission model
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v2.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

What must be established before implementation?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for capture repository boundaries, config origins, current operation, worktree/index state, graph, remotes, and recovery refs before repair.

content.text: A workshop repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v2.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

content.title: Commit before feedback
content.text: Choose from the stated data before opening the worked model. Record which Repository Triage condition decided your prediction.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: gitrevisions Manual — Controls revision names, ancestry, range, exclusion, reflog, and object selection semantics.

hints: Name the Repository Triage precondition or invariant that the workshop case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A workshop repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v2.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: git status alone captures all state needed for repository recovery.

options.text: git status alone captures all state needed for repository recovery.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.
State the Repository Triage preconditions, preserve earlier contracts, and name the trace or changed-input result that will decide success.

steps.title: Audit the initial evidence packet
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v3.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

Inspect the Handoff Runbook trace for this workshop case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for publish an accessible repository runbook covering setup, workflow, gates, release, recovery, and escalation with tested commands.

content.text: For Handoff Runbook, distinguish a claim from evidence by locating the relevant precondition, trace or invariant, and changed input result.

hints: Name the Handoff Runbook precondition or invariant that the workshop case must preserve.

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact
steps.instruction: Append a safe repository investigation headed "# Evidence: git-handoff-runbook-1r5aqr" with a concrete hypothesis, one bounded simulator command, and an observation that changes the next decision.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Handoff Runbook into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: git-handoff-runbook-1r5aqr
# hypothesis: [replace with one falsifiable cause]
# command: choose one bounded simulator observation
# observation: [replace with the result that changes the next decision]

stimulus.title: Handoff Runbook failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: Experienced contributors do not need repository-specific recovery documentation.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Publish an accessible repository runbook covering setup, workflow, gates, release, recovery, and escalation with tested commands.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Handoff Runbook condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Defend the transferred model
steps.instruction: Write the workshop evidence note for Handoff Runbook: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v6.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Publish an accessible repository runbook covering setup, workflow, gates, release, recovery, and escalation with tested commands.
